title: Beeswax floating candles
subtitle: Simple poured floating candles made with beeswax and pre-tabbed floating wicks
excerpt: Beeswax floating candles are one of the most elegant and approachable candle projects. They need no jar, no container, and no special equipment beyond a silicone mould and a thermometer. Floated in a bowl of water they burn steadily for 3 to 5 hours.
sourceNotes: Floating candle method from standard candle-making references. Beeswax properties from British Beekeepers Association technical notes.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Beeswax pours cleanly and re-melts well if you have leftovers.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Can be made weeks or months ahead. Store flat in a tin or box.
recipe.temperatureNote: Melt beeswax to 70 to 75 degrees Celsius. Pour at 65 to 70 degrees Celsius.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 5 years or more. Beeswax is exceptionally stable. Discard only if the candle has absorbed strong odours from storage.

glossaryTerms.definition: A natural wax produced by honeybees, used as the structural material for honeycombs. It has a melting point of around 62 to 65 degrees Celsius, a characteristic honey scent, and burns longer and cleaner than paraffin at an equivalent weight.
A short pre-tabbed wick on a metal base designed to sit upright and float on water. The tab keeps the wick centred and prevents it from tipping as the candle floats.

A white, powdery or frosted film that sometimes appears on the surface of beeswax candles as they age. It is caused by migration of certain fatty acids to the surface and does not affect burn quality. It can be buffed away with a soft cloth.

content.text: has a natural honey scent that is enough fragrance for most floating candles. Adding essential oils is possible but not necessary. The hardness of beeswax means these candles hold their shape well in a warm room and burn slowly. Placed in a bowl of water, a
candle will burn for 3 to 5 hours before going out as the wax reaches the water level. As the candles age you may notice a white
developing on the surface; buff it with a soft cloth before use. Shelf life is 5 years or more.

items.prepNote: Yellow beeswax gives a warmer colour; white beeswax is bleached but still natural

content.text: Melt the beeswax in a double boiler to 70 to 75 degrees Celsius. Place a pre-tabbed floating wick into each mould cavity, tab-side down, centred. Pour the melted beeswax at 65 to 70 degrees Celsius, filling each cavity to the rim.

Leave to set at room temperature for at least 1 hour. Beeswax sets from the outside in and may show a slight depression in the centre. If needed, top up with a small additional pour at the same temperature. Once fully set and cool, press the candles out of the silicone mould.

Store flat in a tin or lidded box away from heat and strong odours. Beeswax is exceptionally stable and candles will keep for 5 years or more. Buff away any bloom with a soft cloth before use. Never leave a burning candle unattended, and do not burn in a draughty spot that could tip the candle in the water.
